name: MRI Lumbar Spine — Likely Approval
description: Patient with documented radiculopathy, 6 weeks PT, positive SLR.
expected_outcome: UHC: ~20% denial, Aetna: ~30% denial, Cigna: ~15% denial
text:
CLINICAL NOTE

Patient: John Smith
DOB: 03/15/1965
Date of Service: 01/15/2026
Provider: Dr. Sarah Johnson, MD — Orthopedic Surgery
NPI: 1234567890
Member ID: UHC-88234571

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Low back pain with left leg radiculopathy for 8 weeks, worsening despite conservative treatment.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Smith is a 60-year-old male presenting with progressive low back pain radiating down the left posterior thigh to the calf. Symptoms began approximately 8 weeks ago after lifting heavy boxes. He describes the pain as sharp and shooting, rated 7/10, worse with sitting and forward flexion. He reports intermittent numbness and tingling in the left lateral calf and foot.

CONSERVATIVE TREATMENT HISTORY:
- Physical therapy: 2x/week for 6 weeks (12 sessions completed) at ABC Physical Therapy — no significant improvement
- NSAIDs: Naproxen 500mg BID for 6 weeks — minimal relief
- Activity modification and home exercise program — compliant but no improvement
- Lumbar X-ray (12/01/2025): Mild degenerative changes L4-L5, L5-S1. No fracture or listhesis.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
- Lumbar ROM: Flexion limited to 40 degrees by pain
- Straight leg raise: Positive on LEFT at 35 degrees with reproduction of radicular symptoms
- Straight leg raise: Negative on RIGHT
- Motor: Left EHL (extensor hallucis longus) strength 4/5, left tibialis anterior 4+/5
- Sensory: Decreased light touch sensation left L5 dermatome
- Reflexes: Left Achilles reflex diminished (1+) compared to right (2+)
- Gait: Antalgic, favoring left lower extremity

ASSESSMENT:
1. Lumbar radiculopathy, left L5 distribution (M54.41)
2. Low back pain (M54.5)
3. Lumbar degenerative disc disease (M51.36)

PLAN:
MRI lumbar spine without contrast (CPT 72148) to evaluate for disc herniation or spinal stenosis causing left L5 radiculopathy. Patient has failed 6 weeks of conservative management including physical therapy and NSAIDs. Neurological deficits are present and progressive. Imaging is medically necessary to guide further treatment decisions.

Signed: Dr. Sarah Johnson, MD
Date: 01/15/2026
